skill.name: mtls-configuration
skill.description: Configure mutual TLS (mTLS) for zero-trust service-to-service communication. Use when implementing zero-trust networking, certificate management, or securing internal service communication.
skill.summary: Configure mutual TLS (mTLS) for zero-trust service-to-service communication. Use when implementing z...
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation skill providing mTLS configuration templates for Kubernetes service meshes (Istio, Linkerd, SPIFFE). All 42 static findings are false positives: external_commands detected are kubectl/istioctl examples in code blocks (not execution), network references are documentation URLs, certificate paths are template examples (not real credentials), and weak crypto flags were triggered by TLS mode names (ISTIO_MUTUAL, MUTUAL) which are standard secure configurations. No malicious intent or security risks identified.

high_findings.title: False Positive: External Commands Detection
high_findings.description: Static scanner detected 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at 18 locations. These are kubectl, istioctl, openssl command examples in markdown code blocks - not actual execution. Users would run these commands themselves as part of mTLS configuration.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Certificate File References
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged certificate/key file paths as sensitive. These are template paths in YAML configurations (e.g., /etc/certs/client.pem), not actual certificates.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Network URL References
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged hardcoded URLs. These are legitimate documentation URLs to Istio, SPIFFE, cert-manager, and NIST - appropriate for a reference skill.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'weak crypto' at 8 locations. The skill uses TLS modes ISTIO_MUTUAL, MUTUAL, and SIMPLE which are standard secure configurations - not weak algorithms.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SQLite Database Reference
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged SQLite path. This is SPIRE server's standard datastore configuration - legitimate operational documentation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded IP Address
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged bind_address. The 0.0.0.0 is standard SPIRE server configuration to listen on all interfaces - not a security issue.

content.value_statement: This skill provides comprehensive mTLS configuration templates and best practices for securing service-to-service communication in Kubernetes using Istio, Linkerd, and SPIFFE.
actual_capabilities: Generate Istio PeerAuthentication and DestinationRule YAML configurations for strict mTLS
Provide cert-manager integration templates for automated certificate issuance and rotation
Offer SPIFFE/SPIRE server and agent configurations for workload identity
Include Linkerd mTLS setup with automatic certificate management
Supply debugging commands for TLS handshake issues and certificate verification
Document certificate rotation procedures for ongoing security maintenance
limitations: Does not execute Kubernetes commands - only provides configuration templates
Does not manage live certificate lifecycle - users must implement automation
Requires existing Kubernetes cluster with service mesh installed
Does not cover client-side mTLS outside of service mesh environments

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Setting Up Service Mesh Security
use_cases.description: Configure strict mTLS for all services in an Istio service mesh to meet PCI-DSS compliance requirements.
use_cases.target_user: DevOps and platform engineers implementing zero-trust networking

use_cases.title: Security Architect Designing Certificate Authority
use_cases.description: Design a certificate hierarchy using cert-manager with Istio integration for automated rotation.
use_cases.target_user: Security architects planning certificate management strategies

use_cases.title: Developer Troubleshooting mTLS Handshake Failures
use_cases.description: Diagnose why service-to-service calls are failing with TLS errors using provided debugging commands.
use_cases.target_user: Developers debugging connectivity issues in microservices

prompt_templates.title: Basic mTLS Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I enable strict mTLS mesh-wide in Istio? Show the YAML configuration needed.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to enable strict mTLS for their entire service mesh

prompt_templates.title: External Service mTLS
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure mTLS for connecting to an external partner API. The partner provides their CA certificate.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to connect to external services with mutual TLS

prompt_templates.title: Certificate Rotation
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up automatic certificate rotation using cert-manager with a 24-hour certificate lifetime.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to implement short-lived certificates with automated rotation

prompt_templates.title: Debug TLS Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: My service is getting TLS handshake errors. Show me how to debug mTLS issues in Istio.
prompt_templates.scenario: User is experiencing mTLS connectivity problems

output_examples.input: How do I enable strict mTLS in Istio?
output_examples.output: To enable strict mTLS mesh-wide in Istio, apply a PeerAuthentication resource...
For external services, use DestinationRule with SIMPLE or MUTUAL TLS mode...
Use istioctl authn tls-check to verify mTLS status, then check peer authentication...

best_practices: Start with PERMISSIVE mode and gradually migrate to STRICT to avoid service disruptions
Use short-lived certificates (24 hours or less) to limit exposure from compromised keys
Monitor certificate expiry and set up automated alerts before rotation deadlines
Implement proper CA hierarchy with root CA and intermediate CAs for isolation
anti_patterns: Disabling mTLS in production for convenience - this defeats the zero-trust security model
Using self-signed certificates without proper CA hierarchy - difficult to manage and audit
Ignoring certificate expiry dates - leads to service outages when certificates expire
Skipping TLS verification to fix connectivity issues - creates security vulnerabilities

faq.question: What is mTLS?
faq.answer: Mutual TLS (mTLS) is a security protocol where both client and server authenticate each other using certificates. It ensures that only authorized services can communicate.

faq.question: Do I need a service mesh for mTLS?
faq.answer: Service meshes like Istio and Linkerd automate mTLS by using sidecar proxies. You can also implement mTLS manually using service libraries, but it requires more configuration.

faq.question: How often should I rotate certificates?
faq.answer: Best practice is to use short-lived certificates (24 hours or less) with automated rotation. This limits the window of exposure if a certificate is compromised.

faq.question: What is the difference between PERMISSIVE and STRICT mTLS?
faq.answer: PERMISSIVE allows both mTLS and plain-text traffic. STRICT requires mTLS for all traffic. Use PERMISSIVE during migration, then switch to STRICT.

faq.question: How do I debug mTLS issues?
faq.answer: Use istioctl authn tls-check to verify mTLS status, check PeerAuthentication and DestinationRule configurations, and review TLS errors in proxy logs.

faq.question: Can mTLS work across clusters?
faq.answer: Yes, you can configure cross-cluster mTLS using shared CA or mesh federation. SPIFFE/SPIRE provides workload identity that works across cluster boundaries.
